unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: This is the last unit of Year 2, and it takes you into a busy city. You learn the names of city places such as the library, the market, the shopping center and the Ferris wheel, and the words you need to get around: bus, underground, ferry, map, directions and straight ahead. At the city aquarium you meet the octopus, the penguin, the turtle and the shark, and describe them with words like amazing, clever, huge and beautiful. You say what you like and don't like, give true facts in the present simple, and talk about what you did yesterday and what you will do tomorrow, just like Amal and her brother Adam in this unit's story, “A Big Day in the City”.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher lecture, then learn one vocabulary group each day and say every word out loud.
Work through the six grammar practices: likes and dislikes, the present simple for facts, and the past and the future.
Read the poem “At the Zebra Crossing” and the aquarium schedule, listen to “The City from the Sky”, then read the story “A Big Day in the City” and answer the twenty-one comprehension questions.
Complete the six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions in each of the two weeks.
Finish with the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.
unit.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 2 Unit 9 source pack under docs/lesson-authoring-super-prompt.md

visual.alt: Cartoon children exploring a city with a market, a library, a ferry on the river, a Ferris wheel and an aquarium tank of sea animals.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A building where you can read books and borrow them to take home.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I go to the library to borrow books about the sea.
practiceSentences: Our city library opens at nine o'clock every morning.
Which shelf holds the animal books in the library?
“The library is my favourite place,” said Adam.
I return my library book every Saturday.
The library near the market has a quiet reading corner.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - i - b - r - a - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with an adult, tell the tutor one book you would borrow from the library, and ask it to check your sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A large building with many different shops inside it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We bought new school shoes at the shopping center.
practiceSentences: The shopping center has fifty shops on three floors.
Is the shopping center open on Sunday?
“Meet me by the shopping center doors,” said Mum.
I always get lost inside the big shopping center.
A glass lift rises to the top of the shopping center.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - h - o - p - p - i - n - g - space - c - e - n - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper beside you, name three shops you would find in a shopping center and ask the tutor to add two more.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A train that runs in tunnels below the city streets.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We took the underground to the museum.
practiceSentences: The underground stops at ten stations on our line.
Does the underground go all the way to the aquarium?
“Hold my hand on the underground,” said Dad.
I like counting the stops on the underground.
Deep below the busy road, the underground rumbles along.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: u - n - d - e - r - g - r - o - u - n - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor, with an adult present, to explain how the underground can run below a busy road, then say the answer back in your own words.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A boat that carries people and cars across water.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We took the ferry across the wide river.
practiceSentences: The ferry leaves the dock at eight o'clock.
When does the next ferry come back?
“Look, the ferry is here!” shouted Amal.
I wave to the seagulls from the ferry deck.
On a windy day the ferry rocks gently.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - e - r - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult listening, tell the tutor what you would see from a ferry and ask it for one more word about boats.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very big wheel at a park that you ride for fun.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We saw the whole city from the Ferris wheel.
practiceSentences: The Ferris wheel turns slowly against the blue sky.
Is the Ferris wheel taller than the library?
“The Ferris wheel is my favourite ride,” said Nora.
I felt brave at the top of the Ferris wheel.
At night the Ferris wheel shines with coloured lights.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: F - e - r - r - i - s - space - w - h - e - e - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Describe the view from a Ferris wheel to the tutor while your helper sits with you, and ask it to suggest one better describing word.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A busy place where people buy and sell food and other things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Mum buys fruit and fish at the market.
practiceSentences: The market opens early and closes at sunset.
How much does one mango cost at the market?
“Carry the basket for me,” said Omar at the market.
I love the smell of fresh bread at the market.
Bright cloth hangs above every stall in the market.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - r - k - e - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor three things people sell at a market, with an adult beside you, and ask it to name one thing you forgot.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: All the cars, buses and lorries moving on the roads.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There is a lot of traffic outside the shopping center.
practiceSentences: The traffic moves slowly when heavy rain falls.
Why is the traffic so loud this morning?
“Wait for the traffic to stop,” said Adam.
I cover my ears when the traffic roars past.
From the tall building, the traffic looks tiny and slow.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - r - a - f - f - i - c
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, describe the traffic near your home to the tutor and ask it for two sound words you could use.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A flying machine that can go straight up and hover in one place.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We saw the whole city from a helicopter.
practiceSentences: The helicopter lands on the roof of the hospital.
Can a helicopter fly backwards?
“A helicopter is over the park!” called Leo.
I hear a helicopter above our street every evening.
A helicopter carries doctors to people who need help quickly.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - e - l - i - c - o - p - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor, while your helper listens, how a helicopter is different from a plane, then say the difference in one sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A long vehicle that carries many people along the road.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We take the bus to school every morning.
practiceSentences: The school bus is bright yellow and very noisy.
Which bus goes to the aquarium?
“Our bus is coming,” said Maya.
I sit near the window on the bus.
Twenty people waited at the bus stop in the rain.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor where your bus stop is and what you see there, with an adult sitting with you.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A drawing that shows you where places are.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We used a map to find the hospital.
practiceSentences: The map shows every street in our town.
Can you find the ferry on this map?
“Turn the map the right way up,” laughed Adam.
I keep a small map in my school bag.
A red star on the map marks the library.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - p
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Draw a simple map of your street, then describe it to the tutor while an adult checks your words.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Words that tell you which way to go.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A kind woman gave us directions to the market.
practiceSentences: Good directions help a lost person find the way.
Can you give me directions to the park?
“Listen to my directions carefully,” said Nora.
I follow Dad's directions all the way to the shop.
The sign gives directions to the aquarium and the ferry.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - r - e - c - t - i - o - n - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Give the tutor directions from your front door to your kitchen, with an adult beside you, and ask it to repeat them back.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Going forward without turning to the left or the right.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Walk straight ahead to the traffic light.
practiceSentences: The hospital is straight ahead at the end of this road.
Do I go straight ahead or turn left here?
“Keep straight ahead until you see the library,” said Adam.
I ran straight ahead across the empty playground.
Straight ahead of the ferry stood the tall city buildings.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - r - a - i - g - h - t - space - a - h - e - a - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to give you three moves that use straight ahead, turn left and turn right, and follow them safely with an adult in the room.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A building where sea animals live in big glass tanks.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We saw colourful fish at the aquarium.
practiceSentences: The aquarium opens its doors at ten o'clock.
Which animal shall we see first at the aquarium?
“The aquarium is dark and cool,” whispered Mina.
I pressed my nose against the aquarium glass.
One city aquarium can hold thousands of sea creatures.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - q - u - a - r - i - u - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, tell the tutor which aquarium animal you would visit first and why.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A sea animal with eight long arms and no bones at all.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The octopus can change its colour to hide.
practiceSentences: An octopus has eight strong arms.
How does an octopus squeeze through a tiny hole?
“Look at that clever octopus,” said Sami.
I watched the octopus curl around a grey rock.
The octopus show starts at ten o'clock every day.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: o - c - t - o - p - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor why people call the octopus clever, with your helper listening, and then explain it back in two sentences.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A black-and-white bird that swims well but cannot fly.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Penguins waddle from side to side when they walk.
practiceSentences: A penguin swims much faster than it walks.
Why can a penguin not fly?
“The penguin is sliding on its tummy,” laughed Theo.
I could watch the penguin feeding all day.
Every penguin at the aquarium has its own name.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - e - n - g - u - i - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor two things a penguin can do and one thing it cannot do, while an adult checks your sentences.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A slow sea animal with a hard shell on its back.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The turtle pulled its head into its shell.
practiceSentences: A turtle can live for a very long time.
Is that turtle bigger than me?
“The turtle is huge!” cried Daniel.
I drew a green turtle in my notebook.
Turtle Time at the aquarium starts after lunch.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - u - r - t - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor how a turtle uses its shell, with an adult beside you, then draw the shell and label it.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A big fish with sharp teeth that lives in the sea.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Some sharks have very sharp teeth.
practiceSentences: A shark swims fast and never stops moving.
Does every shark live in deep water?
“That shark looks hungry,” said Adam quietly.
I was not afraid of the shark behind the thick glass.
The shark feeding happens at twelve o'clock.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - h - a - r - k
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask the tutor for two true facts about a shark, then choose the fact that surprised you most.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: So good or so surprising that you want to tell everybody.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The dolphin show at the aquarium was amazing.
practiceSentences: The view from the Ferris wheel is amazing.
Was the octopus show really that amazing?
“What an amazing city!” said Amal.
I told Mum about my amazing day by the river.
Amazing colours filled the sky above the water.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - m - a - z - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor about one amazing thing you saw this week, with an adult listening, and ask whether your sentence sounds excited.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Very lovely to look at or to listen to.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The colourful fish at the aquarium are beautiful.
practiceSentences: The city looks beautiful from the top of the wheel.
Is the green turtle the most beautiful animal here?
“Your painting is beautiful,” said Hana.
I picked a beautiful shell beside the river.
Beautiful music played inside the shopping center.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - a - u - t - i - f - u - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Describe something beautiful in your home to the tutor while an adult sits with you, and ask it for one more describing word.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Quick to learn new things and to solve problems.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Octopuses are very clever animals.
practiceSentences: A clever animal can find food in a new place.
Which animal at the aquarium is the most clever?
“That was a clever idea,” said Adam.
I felt clever when I read the city map by myself.
Clever crows drop nuts onto the road for cars to crack.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - l - e - v - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to describe one clever thing an animal can do, with an adult present, then tell it about a clever thing you did.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Able to hurt you, so you must be careful near it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Crossing a busy road can be dangerous.
practiceSentences: Heavy traffic is dangerous for a small child.
Is a shark dangerous to people?
“Stop, that road is dangerous,” warned Mum.
I always hold a hand near dangerous traffic.
Deep water can be dangerous for someone who cannot swim.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - a - n - g - e - r - o - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, tell the tutor two places where you must be careful, and ask it for one safety rule for each.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Much bigger than usual, so big that it surprises you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The whale is a huge sea animal.
practiceSentences: The market was huge and full of people.
How did that huge ship fit into the harbour?
“That building is huge,” said Leo.
I stood beside a huge stone lion in the park.
Huge grey clouds covered the whole city.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - u - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Name the biggest thing you can see from your window and tell the tutor why it is huge, with an adult listening.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Making you feel afraid, even when you are quite safe.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Some people think sharks are scary.
practiceSentences: The dark tunnel of the underground felt scary.
Is the shark tank scary or exciting?
“The loud traffic is scary,” said the old man.
I hold Dad's hand when a scary story begins.
Scary stories are more fun when somebody reads them to you.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - c - a - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor about something that once felt scary and then felt fine, with an adult sitting beside you.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name common places in a city, such as the library, shopping center, underground and ferry.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Six city places named correctly in Activity 1, and three places described aloud in the Speaking 1 recording.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about city sounds and the things you see, such as traffic and a helicopter.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: The city sights named in the story and listening questions, plus six sentences in Writing 4 that use Group 2 words.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say what you like and don't like, and agree or disagree politely.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Ten polite replies in Speaking 2, and six written opinions with agreeing or disagreeing replies in Activity 6.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Describe animals and places using adjectives such as amazing, clever, huge and beautiful.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Three adjective clues used in the Mystery Animal game, and three describing words used in the Writing 5 animal report.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use the present simple to state facts, such as “Octopuses have eight arms.”
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct verb forms in Grammar Practice 2, and eight true facts written on the aquarium fact cards in Grammar 5.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk and write about the past and the future, such as “We went to the market. Tomorrow we will ride the ferry.”
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four correct past and future forms in Grammar Practice 3, and three past plus three future sentences in Writing 6.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Follow and give simple directions to get around a city.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: The map route followed correctly in Activity 4, and six clear directions given in the Speaking 4 recording.

readings.title: A Poem: “At the Zebra Crossing”
readings.passageScript:
A zebra crossing is a safe place to cross the road. Read this poem:
At the zebra crossing,
Look around at city places.
Look around at city faces.
I bend down to tie my laces… Oops!
WALK! Hurry up! Let's cross!

readings.title: The Aquarium Schedule
readings.passageScript:
Read this schedule from the city aquarium:
Meet our amazing animals!
10:00 — Octopus Show (They're clever!)
11:00 — Penguin Feeding (They're beautiful!)
12:00 — Shark Feeding (They're hungry!)
2:00 — Turtle Time (They're huge!)

readings.title: The City from the Sky
readings.passageScript:
Leo and his dad take a helicopter ride above the city. Ask an adult to read it aloud, then read it yourself.
“Pilot: From up here you can see the whole city! Leo: Wow, look at all the traffic on the roads! Pilot: That tall building is the library. Leo: I love the library! Pilot: And there is the shopping center. Dad: I like shopping. Leo: I don't! Pilot: The ferry takes people across the river. Leo: Look, I can see the Ferris wheel in the park!”

readings.title: A Big Day in the City
readings.theme: Exploring a city, getting around, and helping a stranger who is lost.
readings.setting: A busy town by the sea in East Africa.
readings.passageScript:
On Saturday morning, Amal took the bus into the city. Her big brother Adam went with her. It was Amal's first big day of exploring. Her eyes were wide with wonder.
“Look at all the traffic!” said Amal. Cars and buses filled the roads. A helicopter flew high above the tall buildings.
“The city is amazing,” said Adam. “Come, I will show you the market first.”
The market was huge and busy. People were buying fish, fruit and bright cloth. Amal smelled fresh bread and sweet mangoes.
“I like the market,” said Amal happily.
“I do too!” said Adam. “Now let's go to the library. It is my favourite place.”
But near the shopping center they saw an old man. He looked worried. He held a small map. He turned it this way and that.
“Excuse me,” the old man said kindly. “I am lost. Can you help me? I need to find the hospital. My daughter is there with a new baby.”
Amal looked at Adam. Their mother always said, “A kind person helps a stranger.”
“We can help you,” said Adam. “The hospital is not far.”
Adam pointed down the road. “Walk straight ahead to the traffic light,” he said. “Then turn left. Walk to the next street. The hospital is on your right. It is a big white building.”
The old man smiled. His face still looked a little worried. “Thank you,” he said. “But my legs are slow, and the traffic is scary.”
“Then we will walk with you,” said Amal.
So the three of them walked together. They crossed the road carefully at the zebra crossing. They walked straight ahead and turned left. There stood the hospital, a big white building. It was just as Adam had said.
“You are here!” said Amal.
“Thank you, my friends,” said the old man. His eyes were bright now. “You are clever and kind children. Today you helped a stranger. I will remember it for a long time.”
He waved goodbye and went inside to meet his new grandchild.
Amal felt warm inside. “Helping him was better than the market,” she said.
“I think so too,” laughed Adam. “Come — the library is close. And after that, I have a surprise.”
At the library, Amal read a beautiful book about the sea. She learned that an octopus has eight arms. She also learned that a turtle carries a hard shell.
Then came the surprise. Adam took her down to the river. They rode the ferry across the water. From the boat, Amal could see the whole city.
“Adam, look!” she cried. “I can see the Ferris wheel!”
The great wheel turned slowly against the blue sky. The city was beautiful.
That night, Amal told her mother everything. She talked about the huge market and the lost old man. She told her about the library book and the ferry ride.
“You had a big day,” her mother said, hugging her. “And you helped someone who needed you. That is the best thing of all.”
Amal closed her eyes and smiled. “Tomorrow,” she whispered, “I will explore the city again.”

comprehension.question: What does the poem tell you to look around at when you stand at the zebra crossing?
comprehension.correctAnswer: City places and city faces.
comprehension.explanation: Two lines of the poem begin with the same words, “Look around at”, and then name city places and city faces.

comprehension.question: What does the person in the poem stop to do just before crossing the road?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The person bends down to tie their laces.
comprehension.explanation: The fourth line says, “I bend down to tie my laces… Oops!”, so the laces are tied first.

comprehension.question: Which three words at the ends of the poem's lines rhyme with one another?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Places, faces and laces.
comprehension.explanation: All three words end with the same -aces sound, which is the rhyme pattern that holds the poem together.

comprehension.question: The last line is written in capital letters with exclamation marks. Why do you think the poet wrote it that way?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because the words are shouted, and shouting shows there is only a short, safe moment left to cross.
comprehension.explanation: Capital letters and exclamation marks show a loud, urgent voice, and “Hurry up!” tells you the person must move quickly.

comprehension.question: How many animal shows does the aquarium schedule list, and which animals can you see?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Four shows: the octopus, the penguin, the shark and the turtle.
comprehension.explanation: The schedule gives four times, and each time names one animal, so there are four shows in all.

comprehension.question: What time does the Penguin Feeding start, and what time does Turtle Time start?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The Penguin Feeding starts at 11:00 and Turtle Time starts at 2:00.
comprehension.explanation: The schedule lists the times down the left-hand side, so you read across from each time to find the animal.

comprehension.question: The schedule calls one animal clever and another one hungry. Which animal is which?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The octopus is clever and the shark is hungry.
comprehension.explanation: Each line ends with a describing word in brackets, so “They're clever!” belongs to the octopus and “They're hungry!” belongs to the shark.

comprehension.question: If you arrive at the aquarium at 11:30, which show comes next?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The Shark Feeding at 12:00.
comprehension.explanation: 11:30 is after the 11:00 Penguin Feeding, so the next time on the schedule is 12:00.

comprehension.question: What does Leo see on the roads, and which tall building does the pilot point out?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Leo sees all the traffic on the roads, and the tall building is the library.
comprehension.explanation: Leo shouts about the traffic first, and the pilot answers by naming the library as the tall building.

comprehension.question: Leo's dad says, “I like shopping.” What does Leo reply, and what does his reply tell you?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Leo replies, “I don't!”, which tells you that he does not enjoy shopping.
comprehension.explanation: “I don't.” is the polite way to disagree, so father and son enjoy different things.

comprehension.question: According to the pilot, what does the ferry do, and what can Leo see in the park?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The ferry takes people across the river, and Leo can see the Ferris wheel in the park.
comprehension.explanation: The pilot explains the ferry, and Leo's last line names the Ferris wheel in the park.

comprehension.question: How did Amal and Adam travel into the city on Saturday morning?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They took the bus into the city.
comprehension.explanation: The opening two sentences say that Amal took the bus and that Adam went with her.

comprehension.question: What did Amal smell at the market, and what were people buying there?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She smelled fresh bread and sweet mangoes, and people were buying fish, fruit and bright cloth.
comprehension.explanation: The market paragraph names what people bought and then what Amal smelled.

comprehension.question: Who did the children meet near the shopping center, and what was his problem?
comprehension.correctAnswer: An old man who was lost and needed to find the hospital, because his daughter was there with a new baby.
comprehension.explanation: The old man holds a map, turns it around, and then explains that he is lost.

comprehension.question: What three directions did Adam give the old man to reach the hospital?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Walk straight ahead to the traffic light, then turn left, then walk to the next street, where the hospital stands on the right.
comprehension.explanation: Adam points down the road and gives the three steps in order, ending with the big white building.

comprehension.question: Adam had already given clear directions. Why did the children decide to walk with the old man as well?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because the old man said that his legs were slow and the traffic was scary, so he still needed help.
comprehension.explanation: The old man thanks them but still looks worried, and his two reasons show that directions alone were not enough.

comprehension.question: What two facts about sea animals did Amal learn from the book at the library?
comprehension.correctAnswer: That an octopus has eight arms, and that a turtle carries a hard shell.
comprehension.explanation: The library paragraph gives the two facts in two sentences, one after the other.

comprehension.question: What was Adam's surprise at the end of the day?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He took Amal down to the river, and they rode the ferry across the water.
comprehension.explanation: Adam promises a surprise after the library, and the surprise turns out to be the ferry ride.

comprehension.question: Amal said that helping the old man was better than the market. Why do you think she felt that way?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Answers vary. Look for the idea that helping someone who needed her felt more important than shopping, and that it made her feel warm inside.
comprehension.explanation: The story says Amal felt warm inside straight after she said it, which is the clue to her feeling.

comprehension.question: Their mother says, “A kind person helps a stranger.” What is one safe way you could help somebody who is lost near your home?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Answers vary. Accept a safe idea such as telling a trusted adult, pointing to a landmark, or walking with the person only when an adult is with you.
comprehension.explanation: Award the mark when the child names a helpful action and keeps a trusted adult involved.

comprehension.question: Name two things in the story's city that you also have where you live, and one thing that you do not have.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Answers vary. Look for two named things from the story, such as the market, the bus, the library or the ferry, and one that is missing where the child lives.
comprehension.explanation: The child must use words from the story rather than general ideas, which shows careful reading.

grammar.title: Likes and Dislikes
grammar.explanation: In a busy city there is so much to do. We use like and don't like to say what we enjoy and what we do not enjoy, and we can agree or disagree with a friend without being rude.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
I like… tells someone what you enjoy: “I like riding on the ferry.”
I don't like… tells someone what you do not enjoy: “I don't like the traffic.”
I do too! agrees with a friend: “I like the library.” — “I do too!”
I don't. disagrees politely: “I like shopping.” — “I don't.”
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “I no like traffic.” The word don't already does the job of no, so the sentence becomes “I don't like traffic.”
grammar.memoryTip: don't is a short way of saying do not. Put don't in front of like whenever you do not enjoy something.
grammar.practice:
Write a like or don't like answer for each one.
1. riding on the underground → I ______ riding on the underground.
2. loud traffic → I ______ loud traffic.
3. Your friend says, “I like the aquarium.” You agree, so you say “______!”
4. Your friend says, “I like the Ferris wheel.” You disagree politely, so you say “______.”

grammar.title: Likes and Dislikes: A City Opinion Poll
grammar.explanation: Now you use like and don't like on your own. A poll asks other people what they enjoy and then reports their answers politely, even when nobody agrees with anybody else.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Ask the question, then report the answer as a full sentence.
“Do you like the market?” — “Yes, I like the market.”
“Do you like loud traffic?” — “No, I don't like loud traffic.”
Adam likes the library, and Nora does too.
Amal likes the ferry, but Mina does not.
grammar.commonMistake: It is easy to reply to a friend with only the word yes. Saying the whole sentence, such as “Yes, I like the ferry.”, makes your opinion much clearer.
grammar.memoryTip: Two replies do the whole job: “I do too!” when you agree, and “I don't.” when you do not.
grammar.practice: Ask four people at home about four different city places from your word list. Write each person's answer as a full sentence, then add one sentence of your own saying whether you agree or disagree with them.

grammar.title: Present Simple for Facts
grammar.explanation: The present simple tells true facts and things that happen again and again. It is the tense we need for facts about city places, opening times and sea animals.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the plain verb with I, you, we and they.
I visit the library every week.
They take the bus to school.
Sharks swim in the sea.
Add -s or -es when you talk about he, she or it, and about one animal or one place.
She goes to the aquarium on Saturdays.
The library opens at nine o'clock.
An octopus has eight arms.
grammar.commonMistake: Writing “The ferry take people across the river.” leaves out the -s. One ferry is an it, so the sentence needs “The ferry takes people across the river.”
grammar.memoryTip: One thing needs one s. Say it to yourself: it, he, she — don't forget the s!
grammar.practice:
Write the correct form of the verb in brackets.
1. The library ______ (open) at nine o'clock.
2. The ferry ______ (take) people across the river.
3. Octopuses ______ (have) eight arms.
4. Sharks ______ (swim) fast in the water.
5. A penguin ______ (not fly), but it ______ (swim) very well.

grammar.title: Present Simple for Facts: Aquarium Fact Cards
grammar.explanation: A fact card gives true information in short present simple sentences. Choosing between the plain verb and the -s form is what makes every fact sound correct to a reader.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Count how many first, then choose the verb.
One animal: The turtle carries a hard shell.
Many animals: Turtles carry hard shells.
One place: The aquarium closes at five o'clock.
Many places: Markets close when the sun goes down.
grammar.commonMistake: Children sometimes add -s to every verb, as in “Sharks swims fast.” More than one shark takes the plain verb, so the fact is “Sharks swim fast.”
grammar.memoryTip: Count before you write. One takes the -s, and many leave it off.
grammar.practice: Choose two aquarium animals and make a fact card for each one. Write two facts about a single animal, using the -s form, and two facts about the whole group, using the plain verb. That gives you eight facts in all.

grammar.title: The Past and the Future
grammar.explanation: When you explore a city you want to say what you did yesterday and what you will do tomorrow. The past tells what has already happened, and the future tells what has not happened yet.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For the past, many verbs change their shape.
Today we go → Yesterday we went to the market.
Today we eat → Yesterday we ate at the café.
Today we see → Yesterday we saw the sharks.
For the future, use will and then the plain verb.
Tomorrow we will ride the Ferris wheel.
Tomorrow I will visit the library.
grammar.commonMistake: Writing “Tomorrow we will rode the ferry.” puts a past verb after will. Keep the verb plain and write “Tomorrow we will ride the ferry.”
grammar.memoryTip: will always points forward, so the verb after it stays simple: will go, will see, will ride.
grammar.practice:
Write the past or the future form, following the word in brackets.
1. Yesterday we ______ (go, past) to the aquarium.
2. Tomorrow I ______ (visit, future) the library.
3. We ______ (eat, past) fruit at the market.
4. Tomorrow they ______ (ride, future) the ferry.

grammar.title: The Past and the Future: Yesterday and Tomorrow
grammar.explanation: A time word at the start of a sentence tells your reader at once whether you are looking back or looking forward. In this task you use both kinds of time word in one short report.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Open with a time word, then match the verb to it.
Yesterday I took the underground to the market.
Last Saturday we saw the penguins.
Tomorrow I will walk to the library.
Next week we will ride the ferry again.
grammar.commonMistake: Mixing the two, as in “Yesterday I will go to the park.”, confuses your reader. Yesterday needs a past verb, so write “Yesterday I went to the park.”
grammar.memoryTip: Read the time word first. Yesterday and last week look back, while tomorrow and next week look forward.
grammar.practice: Write six sentences about a city day. Use yesterday, last Saturday and last week to open three past sentences, then use tomorrow, next week and one day to open three future sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Talk About City Places
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose three city places from your word list: the library, the shopping center, the ferry, the Ferris wheel or the aquarium. Say three sentences about each one.
Name it: “This is a / an ______.”
Say what people do there: “People go there to ______.”
Give your opinion: “I like the ______ because ______.” or “I don't like the ______ because ______.”
Record all nine sentences, then listen back. Did you name every place, say what people do there, and give a real reason?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, read your three place descriptions to the tutor and ask which reason was the clearest and why.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — I Like, I Don't Like
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Take turns with an adult at home or with your AI tutor. One person names something in the city and says whether they like it. The other person replies politely.
Say “I do too!” when you agree, and “I don't.” when you do not.
“I like riding on the ferry.” → “I do too!”
“I like shopping at the shopping center.” → “I don't.”
Play ten rounds, and swap who starts each time.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to say five city opinions while your helper listens, and reply politely to each one with “I do too!” or “I don't.”

speaking.title: Speaking Game — Mystery Animal
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one aquarium animal but keep its name a secret. Give three clues and let the other player guess.
Clue 1: “It is ______ and ______.” Use two describing words from Group 4.
Clue 2: “It can ______.”
Clue 3: “It has ______.”
Then ask, “What animal am I thinking of?” Swap turns after every guess.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Give your three animal clues to the tutor with an adult listening, and ask it to guess before you tell it the answer.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — City Guide
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Be a city guide for a visitor who wants to walk from the market to the library.
Give six directions in order, one at a time, such as “Walk straight ahead to the traffic light.” and “Turn left at the bus stop.”
Use straight ahead, turn left, turn right, next to and opposite at least once each.
Record your directions, then ask a listener to draw your route and check that it works.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Read your six directions to the tutor while an adult sits with you, and ask it to repeat the route back so that you can check every turn.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Aquarium Expert
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Be the expert who introduces the animals at the city aquarium.
Choose three animals from the octopus, the penguin, the turtle and the shark.
Give two true present simple facts about each one, such as “An octopus has eight arms.”
Add one describing word for every animal, then record your whole talk for your class.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Say your six animal facts to the tutor with an adult present, and ask it to tell you which verbs needed an -s ending.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — Yesterday and Tomorrow
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Tell the story of one city day, first looking back and then looking forward.
Say three things you did, beginning “Yesterday I ______.”
Say three things you will do next time, beginning “Tomorrow I will ______.”
Finish with one sentence about the place you enjoyed most and why. Record the whole talk and play it to your family.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Play your recording to the tutor with an adult nearby, and ask it to name every past verb and every future verb that it hears.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Describe a City Place
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose one place in the city: the library, the market, the aquarium, the shopping center or the Ferris wheel. Write four sentences that answer these four questions. What is it? What do people do there? What can you find there? Do you like it, and why?
writing.modelText: The library is a quiet place near the market. People go there to read and to borrow books. There are tall shelves, soft chairs and a corner for stories. I like the library because I can always find a good book about the sea.
writing.successCriteria: My four sentences answer all four questions, name one place, use at least one describing word, and each one ends with a full stop.
writing.support: Copy the place name from your word list, and say each sentence aloud before you write it.
writing.extension: Add one more sentence that opens with a time word, such as “On Saturday I ______.”

writing.title: Writing 2 — My City Postcard
writing.promptAndInstructions: Imagine that you spent one whole day in the city. Write a short postcard to a friend. Use at least two past sentences beginning “Today I ______.” and at least two future sentences beginning “Tomorrow I will ______.” Sign your name at the end, then draw your favourite city place on the other side.
writing.modelText:
Dear Nora,
Today I rode the ferry across the river. Today I ate a sweet mango at the market. Tomorrow I will visit the aquarium with Adam. Tomorrow I will ride the Ferris wheel as well.
From Amal
writing.successCriteria: My postcard has a greeting, at least two past sentences, at least two future sentences, and my name at the end.
writing.support: Look back at Grammar Lesson 3 for the past forms went, ate and saw.
writing.extension: Add one sentence that explains why you want to do one of your tomorrow activities.

writing.title: Story response: A Big Day in the City
writing.promptAndInstructions: Answer two tasks about “A Big Day in the City”. First write three sentences about a place in a city that you would like to explore, beginning “I would like to visit ______.” Then imagine that you meet somebody who is lost near your school, and write the directions you would give them using straight ahead, turn left and turn right.
writing.modelText:
I would like to visit the aquarium in the city. I would like to see the octopus change its colour. I would like to watch the penguins at feeding time.
Walk straight ahead past the market. Turn left at the traffic light. Turn right at the bus stop, and the school gate is in front of you.
writing.successCriteria: My three visit sentences each name something I would see, and my directions use straight ahead, turn left and turn right in a sensible order.
writing.support: Reread the paragraph in which Adam gives the old man his directions.
writing.extension: Write what the lost person might say to thank you afterwards.

writing.title: Writing 4 — My City Guide
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a short guide to six useful places in your own town or city. Write one sentence for each place, saying what it is and one thing people do there. Put the places you would visit in the morning first, and the places you would visit later at the end.
writing.modelText: Our library sits beside the market on Green Road. People borrow books and read quietly there. The market opens early, and families buy fruit and fish. The bus station carries people to every part of the town. The hospital helps anybody who is hurt or ill. The park has a huge Ferris wheel that turns all evening.
writing.sentenceStarter: Our library sits beside the market
writing.successCriteria: I wrote one sentence for each of six places, said what people do there, and ordered them from morning to evening.
writing.support: Use the Group 1 and Group 2 words, and check every place name on a map first.
writing.extension: Add a subheading above your morning places and another above your evening places.

writing.title: Writing 5 — An Aquarium Animal Report
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a report of six sentences about one aquarium animal: the octopus, the penguin, the turtle or the shark. Use at least three describing words from Group 4, use the present simple for every fact, and give your report a title.
writing.modelText:
The Clever Octopus
The octopus is a clever sea animal. It has eight long arms and no bones at all. It changes its colour to hide from a hungry shark. An octopus squeezes through a hole the size of its eye. Its home is a huge tank at the city aquarium. Visitors think the octopus is amazing.
writing.sentenceStarter: The octopus is a clever sea animal
writing.successCriteria: My report has a title, six true facts, at least three describing words, and every verb matches the animal I am writing about.
writing.support: Take your facts from Reading 2 and from the library book in the story.
writing.extension: Add one sentence that compares your animal with another animal at the aquarium.

writing.title: Writing 6 — A City Day: Past and Future
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six sentences about visiting a city. The first three sentences tell what you did on your last visit and use past verbs. The last three sentences tell what you will do on your next visit and use will with a plain verb.
writing.modelText: Last Saturday we went to the market and bought sweet mangoes. We saw a helicopter above the tall buildings. We ate our lunch beside the river. Next Saturday we will take the underground to the aquarium. We will watch the shark feeding at twelve o'clock. We will ride the Ferris wheel before we go home.
writing.sentenceStarter: Last Saturday we went to the market
writing.successCriteria: My first three sentences use past verbs, my last three use will with a plain verb, and every sentence names something from the city.
writing.support: Check the past verbs in Grammar Lesson 3: go became went, eat became ate, and see became saw.
writing.extension: Add one sentence saying which of the two visits you enjoyed more and why.

activities.title: Activity 1 — Fill in the City Place
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the correct city place on each line. Use each place word only once.
1. People read and borrow books at the ______.
2. The ______ carries people across the river.
3. People buy new clothes at the ______.
4. The ______ is a train that runs below the city streets.
5. You can see the whole city from the top of the ______.
6. You can watch sea animals at the ______.
activities.answerSummary: 1. library 2. ferry 3. shopping center 4. underground 5. Ferris wheel 6. aquarium

activities.title: Activity 2 — True or False (City and Animals)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each sentence. When a sentence is false, write the correct sentence underneath it.
1. The underground is a train that runs below the city streets.
2. An octopus has six arms.
3. Penguins can fly high in the sky.
4. A turtle has a hard shell that protects it.
activities.answerSummary: 1. True. 2. False, an octopus has eight arms. 3. False, penguins cannot fly, but they swim very well. 4. True.

activities.title: Activity 3 — Match the Place to the Activity
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each city place on the left with what people do there on the right. Draw a line between the pairs, or write out the five pairs.
Places: library, shopping center, ferry, Ferris wheel, aquarium
Activities: crossing the river, looking at sea animals, reading and borrowing books, enjoying a ride with a view, buying clothes and other things
activities.answerSummary: library goes with reading and borrowing books; shopping center goes with buying clothes and other things; ferry goes with crossing the river; Ferris wheel goes with enjoying a ride with a view; aquarium goes with looking at sea animals.

activities.title: Activity 4 — City Map Route
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Look at the city map in your workbook. Start at the bus station and follow these six clues, marking each place as you reach it.
1. Walk straight ahead to the traffic light.
2. Turn left at the market.
3. Walk past the shopping center.
4. Turn right at the river.
5. Cross the road at the zebra crossing.
6. The tall building on your left is where you finish.
Then write your own six directions for the walk from the market back to the library.
activities.answerSummary: The six clues finish at the library. Learners' own routes vary, but each of their six directions must begin with a verb and must use straight ahead, turn left or turn right at least once.

activities.title: Activity 5 — Place, Transport or Sea Animal
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Sort these twelve words into three columns headed Place, Transport and Sea Animal.
library, ferry, octopus, market, bus, penguin, aquarium, underground, shark, shopping center, helicopter, turtle
Then write one sentence about your favourite word in each column.
activities.answerSummary: Place: library, market, aquarium, shopping center. Transport: ferry, bus, underground, helicopter. Sea Animal: octopus, penguin, shark, turtle.

activities.title: Activity 6 — Like, Don't Like, Agree
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Read each city opinion. Write your own opinion in a full sentence, then write a polite reply that agrees or disagrees.
1. “I like riding on the ferry.”
2. “I like the noisy market.”
3. “I don't like heavy traffic.”
4. “I like watching the sharks.”
5. “I don't like a crowded shopping center.”
6. “I like the view from the Ferris wheel.”
activities.answerSummary: Opinions vary. Every answer must be a full sentence using I like or I don't like, followed by “I do too!” to agree or “I don't.” to disagree politely.

assignments.title: Let's Explore the City! learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Put together three pieces of work for the end of Year 2. First complete “Writing 5 — An Aquarium Animal Report” and check every present simple verb. Next record “Speaking 4 — City Guide”, giving your six directions clearly and in order. Finally complete “Writing 6 — A City Day: Past and Future”. Check all three against the writing and speaking rubrics before you submit them together. Marking: the teacher awards up to 16 marks for the two written pieces using rub-writing-v1, and up to 16 marks for the recording using rub-speaking-v1.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does the word library mean?
quizzes.options: A boat that carries people and cars across water. | A building where you can read books and borrow them to take home. | All the cars, buses and lorries moving on the roads. | A sea animal with eight long arms and no bones at all.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A building where you can read books and borrow them to take home.
quizzes.explanation: A library is full of books to read and to borrow. The other three options describe a ferry, traffic and an octopus.

quizzes.question: Which word means a large building with many different shops inside it?
quizzes.explanation: A shopping center holds many shops under one roof. A penguin is a bird, a helicopter flies, and directions are words that tell you the way.

quizzes.question: Which of these travels below the city streets?
quizzes.options: the ferry | the Ferris wheel | the helicopter | the underground
quizzes.explanation: The underground runs through tunnels below the streets. The ferry travels on water, the Ferris wheel turns in a park, and the helicopter flies above the city.

quizzes.question: We took the ____ across the river to the other side.
quizzes.explanation: A ferry is the boat that carries people across water. A market and a library are buildings, and a shark is a sea animal.

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.options: Ferriss weel | Feris wheal | Ferrys wheel | Ferris wheel
quizzes.explanation: Ferris wheel has a double r, a single s, and wheel is spelled with wh and a double e.

quizzes.question: How many arms does an octopus have?
quizzes.explanation: Reading 2 and the library book in the story both say that an octopus has eight arms.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the present simple correctly?
quizzes.options: The ferry take people across the river. | The ferry takes people across the river. | The ferry taking people across the river. | The ferry taked people across the river.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The ferry takes people across the river.
quizzes.explanation: One ferry is an it, so the verb needs the -s ending: takes. The other three leave out the -s, use an -ing form, or invent a past form.

quizzes.question: Which sentence talks about the future?
quizzes.options: Tomorrow we will ride the ferry. | Yesterday we went to the aquarium. | We ate fruit at the market. | Last week we saw the sharks.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Tomorrow we will ride the ferry.
quizzes.explanation: Tomorrow and will both point forward. The other three sentences use the past verbs went, ate and saw.

quizzes.question: Your friend says, “I like the library.” Which reply agrees politely?
quizzes.options: I don't. | I no like it. | Why do you? | I do too!
quizzes.explanation: “I do too!” agrees. “I don't.” disagrees, “I no like it.” is not correct English, and “Why do you?” asks a question instead of giving an opinion.

quizzes.question: Adam told the lost man to walk straight ahead. What does straight ahead mean?
quizzes.options: Turn left at the corner. | Go back the way you came. | Go forward without turning. | Cross the road at the crossing.
quizzes.explanation: Straight ahead means keep going forward and do not turn to the left or the right.

liveSessions.agenda: 5 min prediction from the title; 15 min shared reading aloud with attention to speech marks; 15 min story questions 12 to 18; 10 min hot-seating Amal and the old man

teacherNotes.noteType: AI tutor use and device safety
teacherNotes.note: Every AI tutor task in this unit assumes that an adult is present. Never leave a child alone with the device: sit beside them, keep the screen visible, and close the session together. The prompts below come from the source pack, grouped by file. Vocabulary: “What other important places are in a city?”, “What is the difference between a shopping center and a market?”, “Can you help me practise giving directions?”, “What other animals might I see at an aquarium?”, “Why are octopuses called clever?”, “When should I use each describing word?” Grammar: “Can you check my grammar answers?”, “Why do we say she goes but I go?”, “Can you give me five more sentences to practise the past and the future?” Reading and story: “Can you read the city poem with me and explain it?”, “Can you play a listening game about city places?”, “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?” Speaking and writing: “Can you play the Mystery Animal game with me?”, “Can you check if my pronunciation is correct?”, “Can you check my directions and my sentences?”, “How can I make my writing more interesting?”

This is the last unit of Year 2, so plan time to celebrate the whole year alongside the new content. Many children will not have seen an underground train, a ferry or a Ferris wheel, so show pictures before the vocabulary work and accept a local equivalent in speaking tasks. The road-safety thread in the poem and the story is worth a short real-world walk: practise stopping, looking and listening at a real crossing with an adult for every child. When marking the present simple, treat a missing -s as the next step rather than as a mistake, and praise the child for choosing the right verb form for one animal or many. The story carries the unit's value: a kind person notices when somebody needs help, and helping can matter more than the outing itself.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. library 2. ferry 3. shopping center 4. underground 5. Ferris wheel 6. aquarium. Each place word is used once only.
1. True. 2. False, an octopus has eight arms. 3. False, penguins cannot fly, but they swim very well. 4. True. The corrected sentence is needed for items 2 and 3.
1. like or don't like, whichever the child means. 2. like or don't like, whichever the child means. 3. I do too! 4. I don't. Items 1 and 2 are personal, so check only that the sentence makes sense.
1. opens. 2. takes. 3. have. 4. swim. 5. does not fly, or doesn't fly, and then swims.
1. went. 2. will visit. 3. ate. 4. will ride. Check that no past verb has slipped in after will.
City places and city faces. Both lines begin “Look around at”, so both halves are needed for the mark.
The person bends down to tie their laces. Accept “ties their shoelaces” or “ties my laces”.
Places, faces and laces. All three must be given, because the rhyme runs across three lines.
The words are shouted because there is only a short, safe moment left to cross. Award the second mark when the child points to the capital letters, the exclamation marks or the words “Hurry up!”.
Four shows: the octopus, the penguin, the shark and the turtle. Accept the count and any three of the four animals.
Penguin Feeding at 11:00 and Turtle Time at 2:00. Both times are needed.
The octopus is clever and the shark is hungry. Do not accept the penguin, which is beautiful, or the turtle, which is huge.
The Shark Feeding at 12:00. The child must skip the 11:00 show, which has already started.
Traffic on the roads, and the library. Accept “lots of cars” for the traffic.
He replies “I don't!”, which shows that he does not like shopping. Both the reply and the meaning are needed.
The ferry takes people across the river, and Leo can see the Ferris wheel in the park. Both halves are needed.
They took the bus into the city. Accept “by bus”.
Fresh bread and sweet mangoes; people were buying fish, fruit and bright cloth. Award the mark for the two smells.
An old man who was lost and needed the hospital, because his daughter was there with a new baby. Accept “an old man who could not find the hospital”.
Walk straight ahead to the traffic light, turn left, then walk to the next street, where the hospital is on the right. Award one mark for the order and one for all three steps.
Because his legs were slow and the traffic was scary. Award one mark for each reason.
An octopus has eight arms, and a turtle carries a hard shell. Both facts are needed.
He took her down to the river and they rode the ferry. Accept “a ferry ride”.
Personal answer. Award the marks when the child connects helping the old man to Amal feeling warm inside, rather than simply repeating what she said.

Personal answer. Accept any safe idea, such as telling a trusted adult, pointing out a landmark, or walking with the person only when an adult is there. Do not award the mark for going anywhere alone with a stranger.
Personal answer. Award the mark when two things are named from the story text and one is named as missing where the child lives.
A building where you can read books and borrow them to take home. The distractors describe a ferry, traffic and an octopus.
shopping center. A market sells food outdoors, while a shopping center holds many shops under one roof.
the underground. Point out that under- and -ground together tell the child where the train goes.
ferry. Only the ferry travels on water, so the other three cannot cross a river.
Ferris wheel. Watch for the double r and the wh in wheel when the child copies it into the word wall.

Four sentences answering the four questions in order. Approved model: The library is a quiet place near the market. People go there to read and to borrow books. There are tall shelves, soft chairs and a corner for stories. I like the library because I can always find a good book about the sea.
Four reported answers, each a full sentence, plus four agreement sentences. Look for “I do too!” or “I don't.” used correctly at least once each.
Eight facts in all. The four single-animal facts must carry the -s ending, and the four group facts must use the plain verb, for example “The turtle carries a shell.” and “Turtles carry shells.”
Six sentences: three opening with yesterday, last Saturday or last week and using past verbs, and three opening with tomorrow, next week or one day and using will plus a plain verb.
Six directions in a workable order, using straight ahead, turn left, turn right, next to and opposite at least once each. Check the route on the map before awarding full marks.
Six true facts, two for each of three animals, all in the present simple, plus one adjective per animal. Note any missing -s ending for feedback rather than for a penalty.
Three past sentences opening with Yesterday and three future sentences opening with Tomorrow, then one closing sentence with a reason. Listen for a plain verb after will.
Six place sentences ordered from morning to evening. Approved model: Our library sits beside the market on Green Road. People borrow books and read quietly there.
A titled report of six present simple facts with at least three Group 4 adjectives. Approved model title: The Clever Octopus.
Three past sentences and then three future sentences. Approved model: Last Saturday we went to the market and bought sweet mangoes. Next Saturday we will take the underground to the aquarium.
The six clues end at the library. For the child's own route, check that each direction begins with a verb and that the turns actually reach the library on the map.
Place: library, market, aquarium, shopping center. Transport: ferry, bus, underground, helicopter. Sea Animal: octopus, penguin, shark, turtle. The three sentences afterwards may vary.
Opinions vary, but every answer needs a full I like or I don't like sentence and then either “I do too!” or “I don't.” Mark the politeness of the reply, not whether the child agrees.
Eight. Six is the common wrong answer taken from Activity 2, so check that the child has corrected it there too.
The ferry takes people across the river. One ferry is an it, so the verb takes the -s ending.
Tomorrow we will ride the ferry. Ask the child to underline the time word and the word will.
I do too! Remind the child that “I don't.” is the polite disagreement, not the agreement.
Go forward without turning. Ask the child to demonstrate it by walking three steps forward.
